# Statewave — Full Reference

> Statewave is an open-source memory runtime for AI agents. It records events, compiles them into semantic and episodic memories, and retrieves compact, ranked context bundles for LLM applications.

This document is the long-form, LLM-readable reference for Statewave. For the short index, see [llms.txt](https://statewave.ai/llms.txt). For human docs, see the [statewave-docs repository](https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs).

Statewave is a self-hostable memory runtime for LLM apps and agents. It records raw events as immutable episodes, compiles them into typed semantic and episodic memories with provenance, and assembles ranked, token-bounded context bundles ready to splice into any prompt. Storage is Postgres with the pgvector extension. Compilation runs locally with a heuristic compiler or through LiteLLM (100+ providers, including self-hosted Ollama and vLLM).

## Core concepts

- **Subject** — anything Statewave remembers around: a user, account, agent, repo, project, or session.
- **Episode** — an immutable, append-only event record (a conversation turn, tool call, decision, or observation) attached to a subject.
- **Memory** — a typed fact compiled from episodes. Kinds: `profile_fact`, `preference`, `procedure`, `episode_summary`. Each memory carries confidence, optional validity window (`valid_from` / `valid_to`), embedding vector, and provenance back to its source episodes.
- **Context bundle** — the runtime output: a deterministic, ranked, token-bounded set of memories and episodes ready for the LLM. Sections cover task, facts, procedures, history, and episodes, plus provenance metadata.
- **Compiler** — the layer that turns episodes into memories. Heuristic (fully local) or LLM-backed (via LiteLLM).
- **Ranking** — deterministic, inspectable scoring over kind priority, recency, task relevance (word overlap or cosine similarity), and temporal validity. Items are packed into the configured token budget by composite score.

## Architecture lifecycle

1. **Record.** Episodes are written as immutable, append-only rows. They are the ground truth and are never mutated.
2. **Compile.** A pluggable compiler derives typed memories with provenance and embeddings. Compilation is idempotent — recompiling only processes uncompiled episodes — and runs in a single transaction. Sync by default; v0.5 added a Postgres-backed async job queue (`async: true`) for large subjects.
3. **Store.** Episodes, memories, embeddings, and provenance live in Postgres + pgvector. The `memories` table holds kind, content, summary, confidence, validity window, source episode IDs, status, and the embedding vector.
4. **Retrieve.** Semantic search uses pgvector's `<=>` cosine-distance operator (HNSW-indexed). Policy filters apply first; then deterministic scoring; then items are packed section-by-section into the token budget.
5. **Return.** The bundle includes `assembled_context` (a pre-formatted markdown string ready to inject into the system prompt) plus a `provenance` block with the fact, summary, procedure, and episode IDs that backed the response.

## Compiler modes

**Heuristic compiler (default).** Pattern-based extraction of profile facts, procedures, and per-episode summaries. No external calls; fully local. Deterministic output. Confidence scores 0.6–0.8. Covers name, company, location, preferences, repeated procedures, conversational summaries.

```
STATEWAVE_COMPILER_TYPE=heuristic
```

**LLM compiler.** Calls the configured model via LiteLLM. Batches episodes (~6000 chars per batch); concurrency capped at 4 in-flight requests. Runs in a thread pool to avoid blocking the async event loop. On any provider error it falls back to the heuristic compiler so episodes are never silently dropped.

```
STATEWAVE_COMPILER_TYPE=llm
STATEWAVE_LITELLM_MODEL=gpt-4o-mini                      # OpenAI
# STATEWAVE_LITELLM_MODEL=anthropic/claude-3-haiku-20240307
# STATEWAVE_LITELLM_MODEL=ollama/llama3                  # local
# STATEWAVE_LITELLM_MODEL=vertex_ai/gemini-1.5-flash
```

Any LiteLLM identifier works. Self-hosted models (Ollama, vLLM) keep compilation local.

## Ranking

**Policy filter (first gate).** If the tenant has activated a sensitivity-label policy, each memory is evaluated against rules using the call's `caller_id` and `caller_type`. Deny rules remove memories before scoring; redact rules keep the memory but redact content.

**Core signals (always applied):**

| Signal | Range | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Kind priority | 3–10 | profile_fact=10, procedure=8, episode_summary=5, raw_episode=3 |
| Recency | 0–5 | Linear; most recent gets max |
| Task relevance | 0–5 (text) / 0–8 (semantic) | Word-overlap fraction, or pgvector cosine when embeddings exist |
| Temporal validity | -4 to +3 | Currently valid = +3; expired = -4 |

**Support-agent signals (added when relevant context exists):** active-session boost +6.0, resolved-session penalty -5.0, open-issue boost +4.0, action-step boost +2.0, urgency boost +2.0, idle-chatter penalty -2.0, repeat-issue boost +4.0 (or +6.0 if the prior issue was resolved).

**Token budget.** The assembler packs by composite score until the budget is exhausted, reserving 10 tokens of header per section. Content is not truncated mid-item.

**Validity windows.** Each memory has optional `valid_from` and `valid_to`. When `valid_to` is null or in the future, the memory scores +3; once expired, it scores -4 and is heavily down-ranked.

**What is configurable.** Weights and thresholds are constants in `server/services/context.py`. There is no per-tenant or per-call override today.

## Privacy and data flow

| Layer | Where it runs | What leaves your network |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Storage | Your infrastructure (Postgres + pgvector) | Nothing |
| Retrieval / ranking | Your infrastructure | Nothing |
| Compilation — heuristic | Your infrastructure | Nothing |
| Compilation — LLM | Configured provider via LiteLLM | Episode batches (~6000 chars each) |
| Embeddings | Configured provider | Episode and memory text |
| Your agent's LLM | Wherever you host it | Your agent controls what it sends |

**Configurations:**

- **Fully local.** Heuristic compiler + self-hosted embeddings (or text-only retrieval). Nothing leaves the box.
- **Local extract, hosted vectors.** Heuristic compiler + hosted embedding provider. Only embedding text leaves.
- **Hosted extract, local vectors.** LLM compiler against a self-hosted model + self-hosted embeddings. Nothing leaves if both run in your VPC.
- **Default cloud setup.** LLM compiler + hosted embeddings. Episode batches and embedding text leave for the configured provider(s).

## REST API v1

Base URL: `http://localhost:8100` (default Docker Compose). All endpoints accept and return JSON.

**Authentication.** When `STATEWAVE_API_KEY` is set, include the header `X-API-Key: <key>`. When unset, the API is open (development default).

### POST /v1/episodes

Create an immutable episode.

```json
{
  "subject_id": "user-42",
  "source": "chat",
  "type": "conversation",
  "payload": { "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Hello"}] },
  "metadata": {},
  "provenance": {}
}
```

Response `201`:

```json
{
  "id": "550e8400-...",
  "subject_id": "user-42",
  "source": "chat",
  "type": "conversation",
  "payload": { "...": "..." },
  "metadata": {},
  "provenance": {},
  "created_at": "2026-04-24T12:00:00Z"
}
```

### POST /v1/memories/compile

Compile memories from uncompiled episodes. Idempotent.

Synchronous (default):

```json
{ "subject_id": "user-42" }
```

Response `200`:

```json
{
  "subject_id": "user-42",
  "memories_created": 3,
  "memories": [ { "...": "..." } ]
}
```

Asynchronous (recommended for large subjects):

```json
{ "subject_id": "user-42", "async": true }
```

Response `202`:

```json
{ "job_id": "a1b2c3d4", "status": "pending", "subject_id": "user-42" }
```

### GET /v1/memories/compile/{job_id}

Poll an async compile job. Statuses: `pending`, `running`, `completed`, `failed`. Jobs retained 7 days by default.

```json
{
  "job_id": "a1b2c3d4",
  "status": "completed",
  "subject_id": "user-42",
  "memories_created": 5
}
```

### GET /v1/memories/search

Query string parameters: `subject_id` (required), `kind` (optional), `q` (optional), `semantic` (default `false`), `limit` (default 20, max 100).

```json
{ "memories": [ { "...": "..." } ] }
```

### POST /v1/context

Assemble a ranked, token-bounded context bundle.

```json
{
  "subject_id": "user-42",
  "task": "Help the user with billing",
  "max_tokens": 4000,
  "session_id": "sess-xyz",
  "emit_receipt": true,
  "caller_id": "agent-7",
  "caller_type": "support_agent"
}
```

Response `200`:

```json
{
  "subject_id": "user-42",
  "task": "Help the user with billing",
  "facts": [ { "...": "..." } ],
  "episodes": [ { "...": "..." } ],
  "procedures": [ { "...": "..." } ],
  "provenance": {
    "fact_ids": ["..."],
    "summary_ids": ["..."],
    "procedure_ids": ["..."],
    "episode_ids": ["..."]
  },
  "assembled_context": "## Task\nHelp the user...\n\n## About this user\n- ...",
  "token_estimate": 312,
  "receipt_id": "01ARZ3NDEKTSV4RRFFQ69G5FAV",
  "receipt_emitted": true
}
```

### GET /v1/timeline

Full chronological timeline for a subject. Query: `subject_id` (required).

```json
{ "subject_id": "user-42", "episodes": [], "memories": [] }
```

### GET /v1/subjects/{subject_id}/health

Explainable customer health score. States: `healthy` (≥70), `watch` (40–69), `at_risk` (<40).

```json
{
  "subject_id": "user-42",
  "score": 55,
  "state": "watch",
  "factors": [
    { "signal": "unresolved_issues", "impact": -15, "detail": "1 open session(s)" }
  ]
}
```

### GET /v1/subjects/{subject_id}/sla

SLA metrics — first-response time, resolution time, breach flags per session. Query: `first_response_threshold_minutes` (default 5.0), `resolution_threshold_hours` (default 24.0).

### POST /v1/handoff

Generate a compact handoff pack for escalation.

```json
{
  "subject_id": "user-42",
  "session_id": "sess-abc",
  "reason": "escalation to billing specialist",
  "max_tokens": 4000
}
```

Returns a structured pack with `customer_summary`, `active_issue`, `attempted_steps`, `key_facts`, `resolution_history`, `recent_context`, and a ready-to-paste `handoff_notes` markdown block.

### DELETE /v1/subjects/{subject_id}

Permanent deletion of all episodes and memories for a subject.

```json
{ "subject_id": "user-42", "episodes_deleted": 5, "memories_deleted": 12 }
```

### GET /healthz and GET /readyz

Liveness and readiness probes. `/readyz` returns per-component checks: database, queue, LLM provider.

## Python SDK

Install:

```
pip install statewave
```

Client:

```python
from statewave import StatewaveClient

sw = StatewaveClient("http://localhost:8100")
# or with auth and tenant
sw = StatewaveClient(
    "http://localhost:8100",
    api_key="your-key",
    tenant_id="acme",
)
```

Main methods:

- `create_episode(subject_id, source, type, payload, metadata={}, provenance={})`
- `create_episodes_batch(episodes: list[dict])` — up to 100 per call
- `compile_memories(subject_id)` — returns `CompileResult` with `memories_created` and `memories`
- `get_context(subject_id, task, max_tokens, session_id=None, emit_receipt=False, caller_id=None, caller_type=None)` — returns `ContextBundle`
- `search_memories(subject_id, kind=None, q=None, semantic=False, limit=20)`
- `list_subjects()`
- `get_timeline(subject_id)`
- `delete_subject(subject_id)`

Governance (v0.8):

- `get_receipt(receipt_id)` — fetch the immutable audit receipt for a context call
- `list_receipts(subject_id, limit=50, since=None, until=None, cursor=None)` — cursor-paginated
- `set_memory_labels(memory_id, labels: list[str])` — sensitivity labels (normalized, capped at 32)

Async client mirrors the same API:

```python
from statewave import AsyncStatewaveClient

async with AsyncStatewaveClient("http://localhost:8100") as sw:
    ctx = await sw.get_context("user-42", task="Help with billing")
```

## TypeScript SDK

Install:

```
npm install @statewavedev/sdk
```

Client:

```typescript
import { StatewaveClient } from "@statewavedev/sdk";

const sw = new StatewaveClient("http://localhost:8100");
// or with auth and tenant
const swAuth = new StatewaveClient({
  baseUrl: "http://localhost:8100",
  apiKey: "your-key",
  tenantId: "acme",
});
```

Main methods (camelCase): `createEpisode`, `createEpisodesBatch`, `compileMemories`, `getContext`, `searchMemories`, `listSubjects`, `getTimeline`, `deleteSubject`, `getReceipt`, `listReceipts`, `setMemoryLabels`.

Example:

```typescript
import { StatewaveClient } from "@statewavedev/sdk";

const sw = new StatewaveClient("http://localhost:8100");

await sw.createEpisode({
  subject_id: "user-42",
  source: "support-chat",
  type: "conversation",
  payload: {
    messages: [
      { role: "user", content: "My name is Alice and I work at Globex." },
      { role: "assistant", content: "Welcome Alice!" },
    ],
  },
});

const result = await sw.compileMemories("user-42");
const ctx = await sw.getContext({
  subject_id: "user-42",
  task: "Help with billing",
  max_tokens: 400,
});
console.log(ctx.assembled_context);
```

## How to integrate Statewave into an agent

This is the full loop — record turns, compile memories, retrieve a ranked bundle, splice it into the LLM prompt. Adapted from [statewave-examples/support-agent-llm/support_agent_llm.py](https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-examples/blob/main/support-agent-llm/support_agent_llm.py).

```python
import os
import textwrap
from litellm import completion
from statewave import StatewaveClient

SUBJECT_ID = "user-alice"
LLM_MODEL = os.getenv("LLM_MODEL", "gpt-4o-mini")

SYSTEM_PROMPT = (
    "You are a helpful support agent. Be concise, friendly, and specific. "
    "Use any context provided to personalise your response. "
    "Do not ask for information you already have."
)

# Past interaction history (the agent's long-term context source).
EPISODES = [
    {"messages": [
        {"role": "user", "content": "Hi, I'm Alice Chen from Globex Corp. We're on the Enterprise plan."},
        {"role": "assistant", "content": "Welcome Alice! How can I help?"},
    ]},
    {"messages": [
        {"role": "user", "content": "We set up SSO via Okta. 12 team members are signed in."},
        {"role": "assistant", "content": "SAML SSO is enabled for Globex."},
    ]},
    {"messages": [
        {"role": "user", "content": "I prefer short, direct responses — no fluff."},
        {"role": "assistant", "content": "Noted — I'll keep things concise."},
    ]},
]

CUSTOMER_MESSAGE = "Hi, I need help adding a new team member to our account"


def ask(model: str, system: str, user: str) -> str:
    resp = completion(
        model=model,
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": system},
            {"role": "user", "content": user},
        ],
        max_tokens=150,
    )
    return resp.choices[0].message.content.strip()


def main() -> None:
    sw = StatewaveClient(
        base_url=os.getenv("STATEWAVE_URL", "http://localhost:8100"),
        api_key=os.getenv("STATEWAVE_API_KEY"),
    )

    # (a) Record each turn as an episode.
    for ep in EPISODES:
        sw.create_episode(
            subject_id=SUBJECT_ID,
            source="support-chat",
            type="conversation",
            payload=ep,
        )

    # (b) Compile memories from uncompiled episodes. Idempotent — safe to recall.
    result = sw.compile_memories(SUBJECT_ID)
    print(f"Compiled {result.memories_created} memories from {len(EPISODES)} episodes")

    # (c) Retrieve a ranked, token-bounded bundle for the current task.
    bundle = sw.get_context(
        SUBJECT_ID,
        task="Help this customer with their question",
        max_tokens=400,
    )

    # (d) Splice the assembled context into the LLM system prompt.
    answer = ask(
        LLM_MODEL,
        f"{SYSTEM_PROMPT}\n\n{bundle.assembled_context}",
        CUSTOMER_MESSAGE,
    )
    print(textwrap.fill(answer, width=72))

    # (e) Record the new turn so the next call sees it.
    sw.create_episode(
        subject_id=SUBJECT_ID,
        source="support-chat",
        type="conversation",
        payload={"messages": [
            {"role": "user", "content": CUSTOMER_MESSAGE},
            {"role": "assistant", "content": answer},
        ]},
    )


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

Key integration points:

1. The payload shape `{"messages": [{"role": ..., "content": ...}]}` matches the conversation format LLMs already use; no transformation is needed at record time.
2. `compile_memories` is idempotent, so it is safe to call after every turn, on a schedule, or only when a session ends.
3. `get_context` takes a `task` string; the assembler ranks memories against it (word overlap or cosine, depending on the embedding configuration).
4. `bundle.assembled_context` is a pre-formatted markdown block — splice it straight into the system prompt; no extra prompt engineering required.
5. The bundle includes `provenance` (fact, summary, procedure, episode IDs) so any decision can be traced to the source episodes that backed it.

## Source repositories

- [statewave](https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave) — core server.
- [statewave-py](https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-py) — Python SDK (`pip install statewave`).
- [statewave-ts](https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-ts) — TypeScript SDK (`npm install @statewavedev/sdk`).
- [statewave-connectors](https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-connectors) — connector ecosystem (`@statewavedev/connectors-*`).
- [statewave-docs](https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs) — docs, architecture, API contracts, ADRs.
- [statewave-examples](https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-examples) — runnable end-to-end examples and evaluation flows.
- [statewave-admin](https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-admin) — read-only operator console.
- [statewave-web](https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-web) — marketing site.

## Scope

- Statewave is self-hosted. There is no managed Statewave cloud today.
- The most rigorously evaluated workflow is support-agent memory: handoff packs, health scoring, SLA tracking, repeat-issue detection. Other use cases run on the same primitives but ship fewer pre-built helpers.
- Ranking weights are currently fixed; per-call weight overrides are not yet exposed.
- Horizontal scaling is supported by design (stateless API, Postgres-backed compile queue, webhook DLQ, distributed rate limiting) but not yet load-tested at high RPS by the project — see the horizontal-scaling deployment guide for the operator-facing detail.
